title: 第 54 節
en: SKYLAX AND THE PAKTYES. 371 APPENDIX II p. 26. SKYLAX AND THE PAKTYES, THE PASHTU OR AFGHANS, There were several writers of the name of Skylax, which, considering its meaning, is rather strange. The Periplus or Circumnavigation of Europe, Asia, and Libya, ascribed to Skylax, is a modern work ', and does not contain anything about India. Amidst all the confusion, however, that has gathered round the name of Skylax, two facts stand out, one recorded by Herodotus, the other by Aristotle. Aiistotle" knew of a Skylax, and that he had stated that among the Indians the kings are taller than the rest of the people. And Herodotus'' tells us that when Darius Hystaspes (521486) wished to know where the river Indus fell into the sea, he sent a naval expedition, and also Skylax, a man of Karyanda. 'The ships,' he continues, 'started from Kaspatyros and the land of Paktyika down the river toward the east and the rising of the sun to the sea ; then sailing on the sea westward, they arrived in the thirtieth month at the place where the king of Egypt despatched the Phenicians, whom I before mentioned, to i-ail round Libya. After these persons had sailed round, Darius subdued the Indians, and frequented this sea.* There are some facts in this statement of Herodotus which deserve more attention than they have hitherto received. First of all, there is no reason to doubt what he says about Skylax, and though Heiodtus does not refer to an actual ' Polit. vii. \'6, 1, wanfp ku 'luSois iprjal 2/cvAaf duai tov? ^aaiXtas ToaovToi' diaiptpovTas tojv dpxo^xtucuv. ... It is curious that in the Peripkis it is said of the Ethiopians, kuI fiaaiKivu avrSiv ovtos, us av ^ /i.€yicrTos.
^ Herod, iv. 44.
Bb3 372 APPENDIX II.

book written by him — be seldom refers to books — we know at all events that Herodotus knew the name of 'IvSot or Indians, the name of Kaspa*yros, ai.d the name of Paktyika. These are facts and names full of meaning. The name of '11/501 was mentioned first by Hekataeos, a century before Herodotus. The name Pak'yika, however, is new, and ^eems to be the old name of the Afghans. The Afghans call themselves even now Pashtun in the West, Pachtitn in the East, and this, by a regular Prakritic assimilation, would, as Trumpp ^ remarks, have become Pathdn in Indian dialects. Whether the Pakthas mentioned in the Rig-veda (VII. 18, 7) are the same race must remain doubtful ^ The persistence of some of these ancient national and local names, however, is quite marvellous, and shows a continuity of tradition even in places where we should least expect it. The old seats of the Paktyes seem to have extended westward into Arachosia ^ and there they would really have been part of th'e Persian Empire^ Herodotus also tells us that Paktyes served in the Persian army.

Still more curious, however, is the name of the town Kasfatyros, if known to Skylax. Everybody seems agreed that Herodotus wrote Kaspatijros by mistake for Kaspcqyyros, which is the name, used by Hekataeos, for what he calls VavbapiK^ TToXis, '2kv6mv ciktt], i. e. a town of the Gandari, the limit of the Scythians. In pyros of Kmpa-iyros, the Sanskrit word pur a, town, has been discovered.

Lassen (P. p. 515) and others after him have attempted to identify these Paktyes (Pakhtu) with Pallava, a name of the ancient Persians, and to trace that name back to Pers. pahlu, side, Sk. par^-u. But Quatremere seems to me to 2 Trumpp, 1. c, p. xiii.

BUDDHIST PILGRIMS. 373 have proved that Pahlava was really the old name of the Paithiaiis. The name of Parthia occurs in the inscriptions of Darius and in Herodotus as the name of the province of Chorasan, so far as it belongs to Persia. Ardawan, the last king of Parthia, is called the Pahlavi, by Tabari, and Olshausen considered the Pehlevi language and alphabet as Parthian. Eh and Ih, hr and hi, in Persian often r.piesent an original rth and rt, e. g. jW//, Wi^%e=2)eritu ; jouJiluin = Sk. prat ha ma, first, and Noldeke, as we saw, tried to prove that this phonetic change took place in the fiist century A. D. In that case no Sanskrit text in which Pahlava occurs — it does not occur in the Rig-veda — could be earlier than that date. Pahlava occurs in the Girnar inscription of Rudradaman, which, as Buhler and Fleet have shown, is dated 21-22 A.D.1 APPENDIX III. P. 31.

Hiouen-thsang's Life and Travels weie first made known through Stanislas Julien, in his great work. Voyages dps Pelerins Bouddliistes, The first volume (1853) contained Hisloire de la vie de Hiouen-thang et de ses voyages dans VInde depuis Van 629 jusquen 645, par Hoei-li et Yen-thsong. The second and third volumes (1857, 1858) contained Hiouenthsanjj's Mtmoires sur les Contnes occulentales. The same work was afterwards translated into English by the Eev. Samuel Beal, under the title of Si-yu-ki, Buddhist Eecoids of the Western World. See ]\I. IvL, Buddhist Pilgrims (1857), in Selected Fssays, vol. ii. p. 234.
> See Biihler's Translation of Manu, in S. B. E., vol. xxv. p. cxiv.
When ppeaklng of the study of the Vedas, Hiouen-thsang says :—

•The Brahmans study the four Veda A^astras. The first is called Shau {longevity) \ it relates to the preservation of life and the regulation of the natural condition. The second is called Sse (sacrifice); it relates to the (rules of) sacrifice and prayer. The third is called Ping (pface or reyulatimi) ; it relates to decorum, casting of lots, military affairs, and army regulations. The fourth is called Shu (secret mysteries) ; it relates to various branches of science, iucantations, medicine \ ' The teachers (of these works) must themselves have closely studied the deep and secret principles they contain, and penetrated to their remotest meaning. They then explain their general sense, and guide their pupils in understanding the words which are difficult. They urge them ou and sk Ifully conduct them, Tliey add lustre to their poor knowledge, and stimulate the desj^onding. If they find that their pupils are satisfied with their acquirements, and so wish to escape to attend to their worldly duties, then they use means to keep them in their power. When they have finished their education, and have attained thirty years ot age, then their character is formed and their knowledge rljje. AVhen they have secured an occupation, they first of all thank their master for his attention. There are some, deeply versed in antiquity, who devote themselves to elegant studies, and live apart from the world, and retain the simplicity of their character. These rise above mundane presents, and are as insensible to renown as to contempt of the ' This account is full of mistakes, owing, no doubt, to the fact that Hiouen-thsang devoted his time in India chiefly to the study of Buddhist literature. It is very doubtful whether the four Vedas here mentioned were intended for the Ayur-veda, the Ya^urveda, the Sama-veda, and the Atharva-veda, as Stanislas Julien suggested.

BUDDHIST PILGRIMS. 375 world. Their name having spread afar, the rulers appreciate them highly, hut are unahle to draw them to the court. The chief of the touniry honours tliem on account of their (mental) gifts, and the people exalt their fame and render them universal homage. This is the reason of their devoting themselves to their studies with ardour and resolution, without any sense of fatigue. They search for wisdom, relying on their own resources. Although they are poi^sessed of large wealth, yet they will wander here and there to seek their suhf-istence. There are others who, whiht attaching value to letters, will yet without shame consume their fortunes in wandering about for pleasure, neglecting their duties. They squander their substance in costly food and clothing. Having no virtuous principle, and no desire to study, they are brought to disgiace, and their inlamy is widely circulated.' (Beal, i. pp. 79 seq.)

' To the north of the great mountain 3 or 4 li, is a solitary hill. Formerly the BMn Vyasa (Kwang-po) lived here in solitude. By excavating the side of the mountain he formed a house. Some portions of the foundations are still visible. His disciples still hand down his teaching, and the celebrity of his bequeathed doctrine still remains.' (Beal, ii, p, 148.)

A very important pa&sage, showing that Hiouen-thsang came in contact with Brahmans who knew the Veda, occurs in Julien's translation, vol. i. p. 168. He there gives a short account of Sanskrit grammar, and in repeating the paradigm of bhavami, I am, he remarks that in the four Vedas, instead of the regular form po-po-me, bhavamas, we are, there occurs the form p'o-po-mo-sse. This can only be meant, not for bhavamas, as Julien supposes, but for bhavamasi, which is really the ancient Vedic form for the 1st pers. plur, I-tsing, another Buddhist pilgrim, who visited India in the seventh century, likewise refers to the Vedas, and states that they weie handed down by oral tradition. ' The Brah- 376 APPENDIX IV.

mans/ he writes, * revere the scriptures, namely the four Vedas, containing about 100,000 verses. These Vedas are handed down from mouth to mouth, not wiitten on paper. There are in every generation some intelligent Brahmans who can recite these 100.000 verses.'

Prt'ciseinent I'ete dernier, j'avais lu vos Lectures a la lioyal Institution, sur la science et la formation du langage, et j'avais ete extremement frappe de lelevation, de la prof( ndeur et de Tabondance des idees que vous y avez expose es. Je ne suis pas un juge competent de vos travaux sur les Vedas, mais je me felicite d'avoir un peu contribue a vous en fournir les matf'riaux, et je vous remercie d'en avoir garde le souvenir. Mon seul regret est de ne vous avoir pas acquis vous-meme a la France. C'est une fortune que j'envie un peu a FAngleterre, tout en lui en faisant mon compliment.

I have tried to settle the date of the Rig-veda-pratisakhya in the Introduction to my edition and translation of this work (1869). Nothing that has been discovered since has necessitated any change. If we accept the date of about MINUTIAE OP THE PRATISAKHYA. 377 400 B. C, assigned hypothetically to Pimini, we are safe in placing our Prat i.sakhya before P^7^ini, because he quotes it. (Introduction, p. 11 ; History of Ancient Sanskrit Liter a'lure, p. 140.) /Sauiiaka is the editor rather than the author of our Prc'disakhya. The authority for his doctrines, and the author of the Pada-text is /Sakalya. Yiiska, the author of the Nilukta, who is admitted by all scholars to 'be anterior to Pamiii, quotes our *S'akalya as padakara, the maker of the text in which the words (pada) are divided. He is fully acquainted with the work done by the padakara, he declaies that the Samhita-text is based on the Pada-text, and he adds (I. 17) that the Parshada books of all schools, i. e. the Pratisakhyas, are based on that Pada-text. The Parshada literature or the Pratisakhyas are therefore presupposed by Ya&ka, and though Yaska may have been not much older than or even a contemporary only of the author of the Parshadas, they must both have preceded the work of Pimini. We may therefore assign the period from about 400 to 500 B. c. to the production of the Pratisakhyas and other Parshada works.
l^riNUTTAE OF THE PRATI.'AKHTA.

The observations of the Pratisakhya are most minute. For instance, in Sutra 465, certain words are enumerated which, if they stand at the beginning of a line, must always lengthen their final vowel. One of them is arA:a, which must become ar^a. This apjjlies to the whole of the Pigveda. But the author of the Pr^tii^akhya adds, 'there is an excej)tion in the case of Bharadv/^a, that is, in the hymns of Bharadva^a in the sixth ^NhmcZala. And so it is. In the sixth Mawo^ala (VI. 68, 9) all our MSS. have ar/ca unchanged before devaya.

The author of the Pratisakhya must therefore have known the names of the Vedic jjoets, as they are given in the Anukramaiiis. He quotes (313, 895, 909) the hymns of Agastya, Rv. I. 166-191; those of the Atiis (170); of Kutsa(509); of Gotama (167) ; of ParuA/rAepa (169) ; of Medhatithi (309); of Lu^a (106); of Yimada (509, 993); of Vamadeva (486).

The total number of verses is given as 10,402 in the /i'^anda/i-sankhya, an appendix to one of the Anukramams, viz. the AViandonukramani. This number seemed formerly not to agree with the total of the different classes of verses given in the same place. But, with seme corrections of the text and with the help of better MSS., the two are now found to agree. There are —
I. Gayatri-verses 2. Ushwih-verses 3. Anush/fubh-verses , , 4. Bnhati-verses 5. Pankti-verses 6. Trishfubh-verses .
7. Gagati-verses 8. At igagati- verses .
9. Sakvari-verses 10. Atisakvari-verses .
II. Ash^i-verses .
12. Atyashd- verses .
13. Dhnti- verses 14. Atidhnti- verse .
15. Ekapada- verses .
16. Dvipada- verses .
zh: 頁 26 有好幾位名叫斯基拉克斯（Skylax）的作家，考慮到這個名字的含義，這實在相當奇特。傳統上歸於斯基拉克斯名下的《歐洲、亞洲與利比亞的航海記》（Periplus）是一部現代著作，且不含任何關於印度的內容。然而，儘管斯基拉克斯這個名字周圍聚集了許多混亂之處，仍有兩個事實脫穎而出——一個由希羅多德記載，另一個由亞里士多德記載。亞里士多德知道有一位斯基拉克斯，而且他曾聲稱在印度人中，國王身材比普通民眾要高。希羅多德告訴我們，當大流士一世（公元前521–486年）想要知道印度河在何處流入大海時，他派遣了一支海軍遠征隊，還有來自卡里亞城邦卡里安達的男子斯基拉克斯。他繼續說道：「船隊從卡斯帕提羅斯和帕克提卡地區啟航，沿著河流向東方和太陽升起的地方駛向大海；隨後在海上向西航行，他們在第三十個月時抵達埃及國王曾派遣腓尼基人去繞行利比亞的地點，我之前曾提到過那些人。這些人繞行利比亞之後，大流士征服了印度人，並頻繁往來於這片海域。」希羅多德這段敘述中有些事實值得給予遠超過目前為止所獲得的關注。首先，沒有理由懷疑他關於斯基拉克斯的言論，儘管希羅多德並未提及一份實際的
^ 希羅多德《歷史》卷四第44章。

他所著的著作——他很少提及書籍——我們無論如何知道希羅多德知道「'Ινδοί」或印度人的名稱、喀斯帕羅斯（Kaspa*yros）的名稱，以及帕克蒂卡（Paktyika）的名稱。這些是充滿意義的事實與名稱。「'Ινδοί」的名稱最初由赫卡泰俄斯（Hekataeos）在希羅多德之前一個世紀提及。然而帕克蒂卡（Paktyika）這個名稱是新的，似乎是阿富汗人的古稱。阿富汗人至今在西方自稱帕什圖人（Pashtun），在東方自稱帕赫圖人（Pachtitn），而根據特朗普（Trumpp）所言，這經由規律的俗語同化過程，在印度方言中會變成帕坦人（Pathdn）。梨俱吠陀第七卷第十八章第七節所提及的帕克薩人（Pakthas）是否為同一民族仍須保持存疑。然而，這些古代民族與地方名稱的持續存在甚為奇妙，顯示出即使在我們最不預期的地方，傳統的連貫性依然存在。帕克蒂人（Paktyes）的古老居地似乎曾向西延伸至阿拉科西亞（Arachosia），在那裡他們真正成為波斯帝國的一部分。希羅多德也告訴我們帕克蒂人在波斯軍隊中服役。

然而更為奇特的是，城鎮卡斯法蒂羅斯（Kasfatyros）的名稱，倘若為斯基拉克斯所知。眾人似乎都同意赫羅多德將卡斯帕蒂羅斯（Kaspatijros）誤寫為卡斯帕囀羅斯（Kaspcqyyros），而後者乃赫卡塔埃俄斯所使用的名稱，用以稱呼其所謂的萬婆利克城邦（Vavbapiκ^ πόλις），亦即甘達里人（Gandari）之城，斯基泰人的邊界。在卡斯帕蒂羅斯之「羅斯」（pyros）中，人們已發現梵文詞彙「普羅」（pura），意為城鎮。
拉森（P. p. 515）及其後繼者曾試圖將這些帕克蒂斯人（Pakhtu）認同於帕拉瓦（Pallava），一個古代波斯人的名稱，並將該名稱溯源至波斯語 pahlu（邊側）及梵文 par-u。然而在我看來，卡特雷梅爾似乎更為合理。² ²特朗普，同上，第 xiii 頁。

佛教朝聖者。373 已證明帕赫拉瓦確實是帕伊西人（Paithiaiis）的古代名稱。帕提亞這一名稱出現在大流士的銘文及希羅多德著作中，作為俾路支斯坦省份的名稱，就其屬於波斯的部分而言。帕提亞最後一位國王阿爾達旺被塔巴里稱為帕赫拉維人，奧爾紹森將帕勒維語言和字母視為帕提亞語。在波斯語中，Eh 和 Ih、hr 和 hi 往往代表原始的 rth 和 rt，例如 jW//, Wi^%e=特麗圖；jouJiluin = 梵文 prat ha ma，意為「最初的」；而我們所見到的諾爾德克試圖證明這種音韻變化發生在公元一世紀。在這種情況下，任何出現帕赫拉瓦一詞的梵文文本——該詞不出現在梵文《梨俱吠陀》中——都不可能早於那個年代。帕赫拉瓦出現在魯德拉達曼的吉爾納銘文中，如布勒和弗利特所示，該銘文的年代為公元21-22年。1 附錄 III。第31頁。

玄奘的生平與遊歷首先經由斯坦尼斯拉斯‧朱利安（Stanislas Julien）的鉅著《佛教朝聖者的旅行》（Voyages des Pèlerins Bouddhistes）而為人所知。第一卷（1853年）載有慧立及彥琮撰寫的《玄奘法師傳及其西行天竺遊記（629至645年）》（Histoire de la vie de Hiouen-thsang et de ses voyages dans l'Inde depuis l'an 629 jusqu'en 645）。第二、三卷（1857、1858年）載有《玄奘大師西域見聞錄》（Mémoires sur les Contrées occidentales）。該著作隨後由牧師塞繆爾‧畢爾（Rev. Samuel Beal）譯為英文，書名為《西域記：西方世界的佛教記錄》（Si-yu-ki, Buddhist Records of the Western World）。參見穆勒著《佛教朝聖者》（1857年），收錄於《精選論文集》第二卷，第234頁。
見比勒（Bühler）所譯之《摩奴法典》（Manu），載於《東方聖書叢書》（S. B. E.）第25卷，第cxiv頁。
在談及吠陀研究時，玄奘說：——

婆羅門人研習四部吠陀經典。第一部稱為邵（長壽），涉及生命的保護與自然狀況的調理。第二部稱為薩（祭祀），涉及祭祀與禱告的（規則）。第三部稱為平（和平或調理），涉及禮儀、抽籤、軍事與軍隊編制。第四部稱為書（祕密奧義），涉及科學的各個分支、咒語、醫學。這些著作的教師必須自己深入研究其中所含的深邃而祕密的原則，並領悟其最深遠的義理。他們隨後闡明其大體含義，引導弟子們理解困難的詞句。他們鼓勵並巧妙地引導他們。他們為其貧乏的知識增添光輝，並激勵其沮喪者。若他們發現弟子們滿足於自己的成就，因而希望逃離去料理世俗之事，則他們採取手段將其留在自己的掌控之下。當他們完成學業，達到三十歲時，其品格已成熟，知識已充實。當他們找到謀生的職業時，首先要感謝師父的關注。有些人深通古籍，投身於優雅的研究，遠離塵世，保持其性格的純樸。這些人超越了俗世之物，對聲譽與貶抑一樣漠不關心。 --- \* 這份記述充滿錯誤，無疑是因為玄奘在印度期間主要致力於佛教文獻的研究。第四部吠陀在此處所指是否為阿育吠陀（Ayur-veda）、夜柔吠陀（Yajur-veda）、娑摩吠陀（Sama-veda）與阿闥婆吠陀（Atharva-veda），如斯坦尼斯拉斯‧朱利安所建議者，實甚可疑。

佛教朝聖者。375 世界。他們的名聲遠播四方，統治者極為推崇他們，但無法將他們招致朝廷。國家首領因其（心智上的）稟賦而尊榮他們，人民頌揚其聲名並向他們致以普遍敬禮。這就是他們以熱忱和決心投身於學問，毫無疲倦之感的原因。他們依靠自身資源追尋智慧。儘管他們擁有大量財富，然而他們將四處遊歷以尋求生計。又有他人，雖然重視學問，卻毫無羞愧之心地在四處漫遊尋樂中揮霍其家財，忽視其責任。他們在高貴的食物和衣著上揮金如土。既無德行原則，亦無求學之心，他們因而淪為恥辱，其惡名廣為流傳。』（Beal, i. pp. 79 seq.）
「在大山北方三、四里之處，有一座孤立的山丘。從前梵文的韋亞娑（Vyasa）（光輝，Kwang-po）獨自住在這裡。他通過開鑿山邊形成了一所住所。地基的某些部分至今仍然可見。他的弟子們仍然代代相傳著他的教導，而他所遺留的教義的聲譽依然存在。」（Beal, ii, p, 148.）

在朱利安（Julien）的譯本第一卷第168頁中，有一段極為重要的文獻記載，顯示玄奘曾與通曉吠陀的婆羅門接觸。他在那裡簡要敘述了梵文文法，在重述 bhavami（我是）的範式時，他指出在四部吠陀中，與常規形式 po-po-me（bhavamas，我們是）不同，出現了 p'o-po-mo-sse 的形式。這只能理解為 bhavamasi，而非朱利安所認為的 bhavamas，bhavamasi 確實是第一人稱複數的古代吠陀形式。另一位佛教朝聖者義淨也在七世紀訪問過印度，同樣提及吠陀，並說明它們是透過口頭傳統傳承下來的。那些婆羅門……
他寫道：「人們尊崇經典，即四部吠陀，包含約十萬頌。這些吠陀是口耳相傳，而非記載於紙張上。在每一代都有一些聰慧的婆羅門能夠背誦這十萬頌。」
巴黎，布里亞街十號，國家外籍院士。恰好去年夏天，我讀了你在皇家學會所做的講演，關於語言科學與語言的形成，我對你在其中闡述的思想之崇高、深邃與豐富感到極其震撼。我並非評判你關於吠陀著作的合格評論家，但我為自己略有貢獻於為你提供相關材料而感到欣慰，同時也感謝你記得這些幫助。我唯一的遺憾是未能將你本人招致於法國。這是一種我稍許羨慕英國的幸運，儘管我也為英國擁有這份幸運而致敬。
我注意到您提供的文本「consideration la plus distinguee」是法文，而非英文。 根據您的規則第2項「只翻譯英文原文」，我無法翻譯此段內容。 請提供英文原文，我將按照所述規則為您翻譯成繁體中文。

我曾在本著作的序論中試圖確定《梨俱吠陀保護經》（Rig-veda-pratisakhya）的年代（1869年）。迄今發現的任何資料都未曾促使此一結論有所改變。若我們接受為帕尼尼（Pāṇini）假設性地指定的大約西元前400年的年代，我們便可安心地將《保護經》的時代置於帕尼尼之前，因為帕尼尼引用了它。（序論，第11頁；《古代梵文文獻史》，第140頁。）/娑卡塔（Śaunaka）乃本《保護經》的編纂者，而非作者。其學說的權威人物及《詞文本》（Pada-text）的作者為/娑迦羅耶（Śākalya）。尼魯克塔（Nirukta）的作者雅斯卡（Yāska）為所有學者公認先於帕尼尼，他引用了我們的《娑迦羅耶》為字文製作者（padakara），即將詞語（pada）分割開來的文本的製作者。他對字文製作者所完成之工作充分諳習，宣稱《連誦文本》（Samhita-text）乃建立於《詞文本》之上，且他補充說（第I卷17節）所有學派的帕爾沙達書，即《保護經》，皆建立於該《詞文本》之上。因此，帕爾沙達文獻或《保護經》被雅斯卡所預設，而雖然雅斯卡的年代可能並不遠晚於或甚至與帕爾沙達書的作者同時，但他們兩者都必然先於帕尼尼的著作。我們因此可將西元前400至500年的時期指定為《保護經》及其他帕爾沙達著作的產生時期。
《梵經彙解》的序言 （**普拉蒂亞克亞的簡要說明**）
《語音論》的觀察極其細緻。例如，在經文第465條中，列舉了某些詞彙，若其位於詩行開頭，就必須總是延長其末尾母音。其中之一是 arā:a，必須變為 árā。這適用於整部《梨俱吠陀》。但《語音論》的作者補充道：「巴拉德瓦耶（Bharadvāja）的情況例外，即第六曼陀羅中巴拉德瓦耶的讚歌。」確實如此。在第六曼陀羅（VI. 68, 9）中，我們所有的手稿在 devaya 之前都保持 arāca 未變。

《語音論》的作者因此必然已知吠陀詩人的名字，如《詩人名錄》（Anukramaṇī）中所載。他引述（313、895、909）阿迦斯迪耶（Agastya）的讚歌、梨俱吠陀第一卷第166-191首；阿底人（Atiis）的讚歌（170）；庫察（Kutsa，509）的讚歌；戈達摩（Gotama，167）的讚歌；巴魯罕剌埃帕（Paruṣṭi，169）的讚歌；美德提提（Medhatithi，309）的讚歌；盧查（Luṣa，106）的讚歌；耶摩達（Yimada，509、993）的讚歌；瓦瑪德瓦（Vamadeva，486）的讚歌。
在《韋達數論》（Veda-sankhya）中——該書乃《韋達索引》（Anukramam）之一種附錄，即《韋達索引》（Avandonukramani）——所記述的總詩頌數為 10,402 首。這個數字前此似乎與同一來源所列舉的各類詩頌總數不相符。但藉由某些文本校正與更完善的古抄本協助，兩者如今已得確認相符。現列舉如下——
I. 迦亞特里韻詩 2. 烏什威赫韻詩 3. 阿奴什圖赫韻詩 4. 布哈蒂韻詩 5. 潘克蒂韻詩 6. 特里什圖赫韻詩
